html:
The physical pinhole model
Light rays from the bright target object pass through the pinhole at the origin and form an inverted image on the rear projection plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
:::{.column-margin}
The world is full of **accidental cameras** that create faint images
often ignored by the naive observer.
:::

### Image Formation by Perspective Projection

A pinhole camera projects 3D coordinates in the world to 2D positions on
the projection plane of the camera through the straight line path of
each light ray through the pinhole (@fig-wallpicture). The simple geometry of the camera
lets us identify the projection by inspection. The sketch in @fig-pinhole_names shows a
pinhole camera and the relevant terminology.

![Coordinate systems. In computer vision it is common to use the
**right-hand rule** for choosing the orientation of the 3D coordinate
axes.](figures/imaging/pinhole_names2.png){#fig-pinhole_names
width="70%"}

@fig-pinholeGeometry shows the pinhole camera of @fig-pinhole_names but with the box removed, leaving visible
the projection plane. @fig-pinholeGeometry shows the definition of the three coordinate
systems that we will use:

-   **World coordinates**. Let the origin of a World Cartesian
    coordinate system be the camera's pinhole. The coordinates of 3D
    position of a point, $\mathbf{P}$, in the world will be
    $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$, where the Z axis is perpendicular to the
    camera's sensing plane (projection plane).

-   **Camera coordinates** in the **virtual camera plane**. The camera
    projection plane is behind the pinhole and at a distance $f$ of the
    origin. Let the coordinates in the camera projection plane, $x$ and

elements: translucent 3D camera box casing
pinhole aperture at the origin (0,0,0) with a glowing camera center marker
physical projection plane at Z = -f (rear of the box)
virtual projection plane at Z = +f (in front of the pinhole)
optical/principal axis (Z-axis) extending forward and backward from the origin
Y-axis (vertical) and X-axis (horizontal) forming the coordinate frame at the pinhole
colorful asymmetric 3D target object (e.g., a bright letter F or a multi-colored arrow) in the world space (Z > 0)
light rays traveling from key vertices of the target object, converging at the pinhole, and extending to the physical projection plane
inverted projected image rendered on the physical projection plane
upright projected image rendered on the virtual projection plane
dimension arrow indicating focal length f between the pinhole and the projection plane

interactions.label: Focal length (f)
interactions.teaches: Varying the focal length f scales the physical depth of the camera box; a larger f projects a larger, more magnified image onto the sensor.

interactions.label: Box opacity
interactions.teaches: Making the camera body translucent allows you to see how light rays travel inside the camera to form an inverted image on the rear sensor plane.

interactions.label: Object distance (Z)
interactions.teaches: Moving the object closer to the camera increases the size of its projection, demonstrating the scale factor of f/Z.

interactions.label: Show virtual plane (+f)
interactions.teaches: Toggling the virtual plane at Z = +f illustrates why computer vision models often place the projection plane in front of the pinhole: it avoids image inversion while keeping the math identical.

demo_steps.title: The physical pinhole model
demo_steps.narration: This is the classic pinhole camera setup. Light rays from the bright target object in the world travel through the pinhole at the origin (0,0,0) and hit the physical projection plane at Z = -2.0. Because the rays cross at the pinhole, the projected image on the sensor is upside down and reversed.

demo_steps.title: Peering inside the camera
demo_steps.narration: Let's make the box completely transparent. Notice how the light rays from the top of the object map directly to the bottom of the projection plane. The physical distance between the pinhole aperture and the projection plane is the focal length, f.

demo_steps.title: Changing focal length (f)
demo_steps.narration: By increasing the focal length to f = 3.5, the projection plane moves further away from the pinhole. This makes the rays diverge more before they hit the plane, magnifying the projected image on the sensor.

demo_steps.title: Object distance and scaling
demo_steps.narration: Now, bring the object closer to the camera (Z = 3.5). The projected image grows significantly larger. The projection scale is dictated by the ratio f / Z: closer objects or longer focal lengths create larger projections.

demo_steps.title: The virtual projection plane
demo_steps.narration: To simplify the math and keep images upright, computer vision models often place a mathematical 'virtual plane' in front of the camera at Z = +f. Compare the two planes: the geometry is identical, but the virtual image is upright and much easier to work with.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Perspective camera positioned at an angle (e.g., [8, 5, 8]) looking down at the origin, showing the spatial relationship between the world object, the pinhole, the camera box, and the internal/external planes clearly.
interactionPlan.notes: Ensure the 3D target object is highly asymmetric (like a colorful 'F' shape) so the user can clearly see the inversion on the back plane and the lack of inversion on the virtual front plane. Use dashed lines for the rays extending through the pinhole inside the camera, and solid lines for the rays out in the world.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The camera is flipped: in the original, the projection plane is on the left and the optical axis Z points to the right, whereas in the generated version, the camera faces left.
A colorful 3D object (F-shape) and corresponding light rays have been added, which are not present in the original minimalist textbook diagram.
The red focal length dimension arrow 'f' is oriented axially rather than strictly horizontally below the camera box as shown in the original.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive demonstration of a pinhole camera with outstanding controls, but the camera orientation is mirrored/flipped compared to the textbook figure and includes extra hallucinated elements.
action_items: Reorient the scene so that the camera box is on the left and the optical/principal axis points to the right (positive Z).
Align the coordinate axes strictly to match the original: Y pointing straight up, Z pointing straight right, and X pointing diagonally up-right.
Adjust the red focal length 'f' indicator arrow to be strictly horizontal below the box.
Add a toggle or option to simplify the visualization to match the minimalist style of the original diagram (hiding the F-target and ray lines).

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactivity are excellent, but the camera orientation is flipped and extra elements were added, which can be corrected by refining the implementation.
action_items: Reorient the scene so that the camera box is on the left and the optical/principal axis points to the right (positive Z).
Align the coordinate axes strictly to match the original: Y pointing straight up, Z pointing straight right, and X pointing diagonally up-right.
Adjust the red focal length 'f' indicator arrow to be strictly horizontal below the box.
Add a toggle or option to simplify the visualization to match the minimalist style of the original diagram (hiding the F-target and ray lines).

attempts.html:
The physical pinhole model
Light rays from the bright target object pass through the pinhole at the origin and form an inverted image on the rear projection plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The 3D target object (the colored 'F') and its projection rays are not present in the original textbook figure.
The optical axis Z points horizontally to the right in the original, while in the initial camera view of the 3D render it points towards the bottom-right.
The pinhole center is a simple black dot on a solid face in the original, whereas the 3D render has a yellow glowing sphere with a torus ring.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive figure with robust educational controls, though it adds extra elements like the 3D object and rays not present in the original static diagram.
action_items: Adjust the default camera orientation so that the Z axis points more horizontally to the right, matching the perspective of the original figure.
Add an option (or set Step 1 default) to hide the 3D target object and projection rays to provide an exact replica of the clean textbook figure before introducing interactive extensions.
Style the camera box material in the default state to look more like the solid gray-green color with dashed back-edges from the original diagram.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactivity are outstanding, but the execution needs visual styling refinements, camera adjustments, and a toggle to hide the extra 3D objects and rays to match the original figure's simplicity.
action_items: Adjust the default camera orientation so that the Z axis points more horizontally to the right, matching the perspective of the original figure.

Style the camera box material in the default state to look more like the solid gray-green color with dashed back-edges from the original diagram.

attempts.html:
The physical pinhole model
Light rays from the bright target object pass through the pinhole at the origin and form an inverted image on the rear projection plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The generated figure includes a colorful 3D target object ('F' shape) and corresponding light rays, which are completely absent in the original textbook figure.
Multiple extra labels are present (e.g., 'inverted sensor image', 'asymmetric 3D target', 'Virtual projection plane') that do not exist in the original image.
The camera box in the generated figure has a semi-transparent wireframe look to reveal internal rays, whereas the original is solid-shaded with dashed hidden lines for the projection plane.
The camera view angle in the generated screenshot places the Z-axis pointing towards the front-left, while the original figure has the Z-axis pointing towards the front-right.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactivity and pedagogical step-by-step progression, but contains heavily hallucinated elements like the 3D 'F' target and rays that clutter the simplicity of the original textbook illustration.
action_items: Provide a toggle/option or step to completely hide the 3D target and rays to match the clean original textbook figure.
Adjust the camera viewpoint so the Z-axis points front-right and X-axis points diagonally up-right as depicted in the original diagram.
Add dashed lines to the rear projection plane edges inside the box to faithfully replicate the hidden line drawing of the original.
Reduce visual clutter from extra labels (like 'asymmetric 3D target') when showing the basic pinhole model view.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactivity are highly rated, but the execution contains hallucinated elements, incorrect styling, and camera alignment issues that need to be refined in the generator.

Reduce visual clutter from extra labels (like 'asymmetric 3D target') when showing the basic pinhole model view.
